title: Homogamy in Educational Level and Parental Social Class in Finland: A Log-linear Analysis
abstract: The relative importance of homogamy in ascribed and achieved socio-economic status in contemporary partner selection is not well known. Few studies have explored to what extent these dimensions of homogamy overlap. To fill these gaps in our knowledge, this study examines homogamy in educational level and parental occupational class in Finland. The study uses excellent register data, which includes not only marriages but also nonmarital cohabiting unions. Log-linear models are applied to analyse homogamy in the unions of 30-year-old women (n = 15,066). The results show that educational homogamy is overall stronger than homogamy in parental occupational class, and people with a higher university degree display the highest rates of homogamy. In the case of socio-economic origins, those from upper-white-collar and farmer families are particularly inclined towards in-group partner choice. Homogamy is not pervasive across all status groups: people from lower-white-collar families displayed no tendency towards homogamy, and the tendency was weak among those with an upper-secondary-level education. Also importantly, multivariate analyses show that educational homogamy and homogamy in parental social class are largely independent of one another. The latter two findings likely reflect the relatively egalitarian context of a Nordic welfare state.
